title: How to save energy in the bathroom
description: What a shower, bath, towel rail and extractor fan really cost at 2026 prices, and the bathroom habits that save a UK household over £400 a year.
blurb: Showers, baths, towel rails and extractor fans costed at 2026 rates, plus the timer trick worth hundreds.
lede: The bathroom is the smallest room in most UK homes and one of the most expensive to run, because almost everything that happens in it involves hot water or resistive electric heat. A typical family puts more energy through the shower than through the kettle, the oven and the washing machine combined, and an electric towel rail left on around the clock can quietly cost more than the fridge, the freezer and the lighting for the whole house. The good news is that bathroom savings are mostly about habits and timers rather than spending money, and the few gadgets that do help cost less than a takeaway.
short_answer: The shower is the big number: an eight-minute electric shower costs about 30p, so halving it to four minutes saves a family of four around £216 a year at the July to September 2026 cap. The other large win is the electric towel rail, where a £10 timer cutting a 200W rail from 24 hours to 2 hours a day saves roughly £419 a year. Everything else in the bathroom, from the extractor fan to LED spotlights, costs pennies and should generally be left to do its job.

sections.h2: Start with the shower, because nothing else comes close
sections.html:
Heating water takes a lot of energy. Raising one litre from mains temperature to shower temperature needs roughly 0.03 kWh of heat, and a shower gets through tens of litres in minutes, so shower habits dwarf every other choice you make in this room.

How much your shower costs depends on which kind you have:

  • Electric shower. A unit on the wall, typically rated 7.5 kW to 10.5 kW, that heats cold mains water on demand using full-price electricity. An 8.5 kW unit running for eight minutes uses about 1.13 kWh, which is roughly 30p per shower at the current cap. Our electric shower cost guide breaks this down by power rating.
  • Mixer shower. Fed from your boiler or hot water cylinder, so usually heated by gas at less than a third of the electricity rate per kWh. An eight-minute mixer shower at 10 litres a minute costs around 22p in gas through a combi boiler, but flows are often higher than that, which is why mixers are not automatically cheaper.

The single most effective change in the whole bathroom is shortening showers. Cutting an eight-minute electric shower to four minutes saves about 15p a time. For four people showering daily that is roughly £216 a year, and a household on a water meter saves a similar amount again on water and sewerage charges. A £5 waterproof shower timer, or simply a four-song playlist, is all the technology this requires.

One habit costs money for nothing: running the shower for several minutes to warm the room before getting in. If the bathroom is cold, heat the room, not the drain.

sections.h2: What your bathroom actually costs to run
sections.html:
Here is what the main bathroom energy users cost at current capped rates, assuming an 8.5 kW electric shower, a 10 litres per minute mixer fed by a combi boiler at 85% efficiency, and a typical 80-litre bath fill at 40°C.

UseEnergyCost
Electric shower, 8 minutes1.13 kWh electricity30p
Electric shower, 4 minutes0.57 kWh electricity15p
Mixer shower, 8 minutes (gas)3.06 kWh gas22p
Bath, 80 litres (gas)3.06 kWh gas22p
Electric towel rail, 200W, 24 hours4.8 kWh electricity£1.25
Electric towel rail, 200W, 2 hours on a timer0.4 kWh electricity10p
Extractor fan, 25W, 30 minutes0.013 kWh electricityunder 1p
Six 50W halogen spotlights, 1 hour0.3 kWh electricity8p
Six 5W LED spotlights, 1 hour0.03 kWh electricity1p
Hair dryer, 1.8 kW, 10 minutes0.3 kWh electricity8p

At 26.11p per kWh electricity and 7.33p per kWh gas, Ofgem cap July to September 2026.

Two things stand out. First, a bath and an eight-minute mixer shower cost about the same, so the old advice that showers always beat baths only holds for short showers or against an electric shower. Second, the towel rail line: per day it can cost four times as much as everyone's showers put together.

sections.h2: The electric towel rail is the quiet money pit
sections.html:
Plenty of households treat the electric towel rail like part of the plumbing and leave it on permanently. The element feels gentle and the rail is small, so people assume it costs next to nothing, the same reasoning our energy myths guide picks apart for set-top boxes and phone chargers. The arithmetic says otherwise.

A modest 200W rail running 24 hours a day uses 1,752 kWh a year, which is about £457 at the current cap, more electricity than many households use for their fridge, freezer, washing and lighting combined. Larger 400W to 600W rails left on around the clock can pass £1,000 a year.

The fix costs about £10: a plug-in timer, or a fused-spur timer if the rail is hard-wired, set to run for an hour or two around your usual shower times. Two hours a day on that 200W rail costs roughly £38 a year, a saving of about £419 for towels that are just as dry. If your towel rail is plumbed into the central heating instead, it already runs on gas at less than a third of the price per kWh, so simply use the radiator valve rather than any electric element in summer.

sections.h2: Cut the flow without cutting the comfort
sections.html:
If you have a mixer shower, the flow rate is a hidden multiplier on every shower you take. Many mixers deliver 12 to 14 litres a minute when 8 is plenty for a good shower. Swapping a 12 litres per minute head for an 8 litres per minute water-saving head saves about 9p in gas on every eight-minute shower, which is over £130 a year for a family of four, and roughly doubles if you are on a water meter. Aerating showerheads and inline flow regulators cost a few pounds and fit without tools, which is why they feature in our quick wins under a tenner round-up.

One firm warning: never fit a flow restrictor or low-flow head to an electric shower. Electric showers already run at low flow because the element can only heat so much water per second, and restricting them further can trip the thermal cut-out or damage the unit.

Two settings elsewhere in the house also shape your bathroom costs:

  • Combi boiler hot water temperature. If you find yourself adding lots of cold at the shower mixer, the boiler is heating water hotter than you need. Turning the tap (domestic hot water) setting down towards 45 to 50°C means less wasted heat and safer water at the tap.
  • Hot water cylinder thermostat. If you have a cylinder, keep it at 60°C. Lower risks legionella bacteria, higher wastes energy. An insulating jacket and lagged pipes pay for themselves within months.

sections.h2: Ventilation, heating and lighting: spend pennies, save pounds
sections.html:
Some bathroom energy use is worth every penny, and the extractor fan tops that list. A 25W fan running for half an hour after a shower costs well under 1p, and it removes the moisture that would otherwise condense on cold walls and windows. Damp air is more expensive to heat than dry air, and a mould problem costs far more to fix than a decade of fan running time, so use the fan or open a window after every shower rather than economising here.

Heat the room only when you use it

A bathroom needs to be warm for perhaps an hour a day, not twenty-four. If the radiator has a thermostatic valve, a setting around 2 to 3 is enough to keep the room aired and the towels off the floor, with the boost timed for mornings. What you want to avoid is plugging in a portable electric heater as the default: at 26.11p per kWh, a 2 kW fan heater costs 52p an hour, so it belongs in the category of occasional treat, not routine. Our heating hub covers the cheaper ways to run the whole system, from flow temperature to radiator balancing.

Swap the spotlight bank

Bathrooms often have four to six ceiling spotlights, and if they are still halogen they are a small heater on the ceiling. Six 50W halogens cost 8p an hour against 1p for LED equivalents. At 90 minutes of use a day, the swap saves around £38 a year for an outlay of about £15 in GU10 LED lamps, just check the packaging for a bathroom-suitable IP rating if the fittings are open to steam.

sections.h2: The bottom line for your bathroom
sections.html:
Two changes do almost all the work. Put the electric towel rail on a timer, worth around £419 a year if it currently runs around the clock, and shorten showers, worth around £216 a year for a family of four with an electric shower, before counting water savings. Everything else stacks up in smaller increments: a water-saving head on a mixer shower (£130 or so a year for a family), LED spotlights (£38), a cylinder jacket and sensible water temperatures. The total spend for all of it is under £40, and none of it requires a plumber.

Leave the extractor fan alone, it is the cheapest insurance in the house. And when the weather turns, run through the rest of the rooms too: the bathroom moves are step one of our winter energy checklist, where the same timer-and-habits approach gets applied to the whole home.

faq.q: Is a bath or a shower cheaper?
faq.a: It depends on length and shower type. An 80-litre bath costs about 22p in gas, the same as an eight-minute mixer shower. A four-minute electric shower costs about 15p, so short showers win, but a long shower under a powerful mixer can easily cost more than a bath.

faq.q: Should I leave my electric towel rail on all the time?
faq.a: No. A 200W rail running 24 hours a day costs about £457 a year at the July to September 2026 cap, while two timed hours a day dries towels for about £38. A £10 plug-in or fused-spur timer pays for itself in under a fortnight.

faq.q: Can I fit a water-saving showerhead to an electric shower?
faq.a: No. Electric showers already limit flow to what the heating element can manage, and restricting it further can overheat the unit or trip its safety cut-out. Water-saving heads and flow regulators are for mixer and power showers fed from a boiler or cylinder.

faq.q: Does the extractor fan cost a lot to run?
faq.a: No. A typical 25W bathroom fan costs well under 1p per half-hour run at current rates, around £1 a year with normal use. Skipping it risks condensation and mould, which cost far more to put right, so always run it or open a window after showering.

faq.q: Are showers cheaper at night on Economy 7?
faq.a: Only if you have an off-peak tariff and the shower uses stored or directly heated electricity. An electric shower taken during Economy 7 off-peak hours, or hot water heated overnight by an immersion in a well-insulated cylinder, can cost half the daytime rate. On a standard single-rate tariff the time of day makes no difference.
